summary: Regulatory monitoring changes for food seasoning/snack compliance include significant high-risk updates. Key high-risk additions: Codex now requires verification for maximum contaminant levels (e.g., mycotoxins, metals). Singapore has reinforced strict compliance for food additive limits (only permitted, no non-permitted) and contaminant limits (general compliance, specific prohibitions like Diethylstibestrol), alongside mandatory ingredient/additive labelling. The US FDA has explicitly listed nine major food allergens, set an action level for total Aflatoxins (20 ppb), defined 'gluten-free' standards, mandated allergen preventive controls, and requires specific labelling for cochineal, FD&C Yellow No. 5, and sulfiting agents (≥10 ppm). Thailand has a new prohibition on certain foods. A high-risk labelling extraction issue in Iran requires manual review. Moderate-risk additions across Codex, Indonesia, Japan, Singapore, and Thailand cover various topics like additive, contaminant, allergen, labelling, microbiology, and salt/sodium limits, all requiring detailed verification. Moderate-risk removals were noted for Bangladesh, China, GCC, India, Indonesia, Middle East, Codex, Japan, Singapore, and Thailand, primarily due to rules no longer appearing in extractions, necessitating urgent verification to confirm status.

changes.parameter: Maximum levels for contaminants (e.g., mycotoxins, metals like arsenic, lead, mercury)
changes.newLimit: Verification required via Codex General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995) and other relevant codes of practice.
changes.notes: Codex establishes maximum levels (MLs) for contaminants in food or feed commodities, which are legally permitted to protect human health. The Codex Committee on Contaminants in Food (CCCF) establishes and endorses these levels.

All food businesses must comply with regulatory limits on food additives.
Food products containing non-permitted additives must not be imported or sold.
Only food additives assessed and permitted by SFA are allowed.
All food businesses must comply with regulatory limits on contaminants.
Food products with contaminants exceeding regulatory limits must not be imported or sold.
Import or sale of meat and derived food containing these residues is prohibited.
All ingredients and additives in prepacked foods must be declared on labels.
Certain foods are prohibited from production, import, or sale. Verification is required to ensure the product is not on this list.
Delisted color additives are listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR 81.10 and/or 81.30).
Prohibited substances are listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Part 189).
The nine major food allergens are milk, eggs, fish, Crustacean shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, and sesame.
This is an Action Level.

The FDA issued a final rule defining “gluten-free” for food labeling. A separate rule (August 12, 2020) establishes compliance requirements for fermented and hydrolyzed foods bearing the “gluten-free” claim.

The CGMP & PC rule (21 CFR part 117) establishes requirements for allergen preventive controls to prevent allergen cross-contact in manufacturing and packaging and to prevent undeclared allergens. Facilities must put written procedures in place.
Identified as allergenic substances that must be declared on the label.
The food source name must appear in parentheses following the name of the ingredient.

changes.notes: The type of tree nut, and the species of fish and Crustacean shellfish must be declared.

changes.parameter: Permitted food additives and their conditions of use
changes.newLimit: Verification required via GSFA Online Database
changes.notes: The Codex General Standard for Food Additives (GSFA, Codex STAN 192-1995) sets forth the conditions under which permitted food additives may be used in all foods. The GSFA Online Database provides all adopted provisions, searchable by additive, functional class, and food category. Users are encouraged to consult the Preamble for interpretation.
